question: What is the nature of the soul?
answer_text:
My dear student, this is the most fundamental question of all spiritual knowledge, and I am very pleased that you are asking it. Without understanding the nature of the soul, all our education is simply polishing the birdcage while the bird is suffering within. Let me explain this most important subject matter.

## Primary Teaching

In Bhagavad-gītā (2.20), Lord Kṛṣṇa gives the most perfect description of the soul's nature:

*na jāyate mriyate vā kadācin
nāyaṁ bhūtvā bhavitā vā na bhūyaḥ
ajo nityaḥ śāśvato 'yaṁ purāṇo
na hanyate hanyamāne śarīre*

"For the soul there is neither birth nor death at any time. He has not come into being, does not come into being, and will not come into being. He is unborn, eternal, ever-existing and primeval. He is not slain when the body is slain."

This is the ABC of spiritual knowledge. The soul is completely different from the body. The body is temporary, but the soul is eternal. The body takes birth, grows, produces by-products, dwindles, and vanishes. But the soul undergoes no such changes. It is *kūṭa-stha*, unchangeable, like an anvil.

## Purport

Now you must understand this very carefully. We are not this body. Just as you change your coat and shirt, the soul changes bodies. But the difficulty is that in this material condition, we have forgotten our real nature. Due to misuse of our slight independence, we have become covered by this material body, and now we identify with it.

The soul is by nature eternally active. It is not that the soul is impersonal or void. No! This is a very important point. The soul is a person, an individual, a fragmental part of the Supreme Soul, Kṛṣṇa. In quality, we are one with Kṛṣṇa—we are also eternal, full of knowledge and bliss. But in quantity, we are minute. We are like a drop of ocean water. The quality is the same as the ocean, but the quantity is infinitesimal.

As stated in Bhagavad-gītā (13.22), *kāraṇaṁ guṇa-saṅgo 'sya sad-asad-yoni-janmasu*—the cause of the living entity's birth in different species of life is his association with the modes of material nature. The soul itself has no death, no birth, but when it desires to enjoy this material world independently of Kṛṣṇa, it must accept a body according to its desires and karma. Under the direction of the Supreme Lord, who sits in everyone's heart as Paramātmā, the soul is given a suitable body by material nature.

The soul is fully aware of its own existence—it is consciousness itself. But this consciousness has become covered, just as the sun becomes covered by clouds. The sun is always shining, but we cannot see it when clouds cover it. Similarly, our spiritual consciousness is covered by the cloud of material desire and false identification with the body.

## Practical Application

So what should you do with this knowledge? First, you must become convinced: "I am not this body. I am an eternal spirit soul." This conviction must be cultivated through study of *śāstra* (scripture) and through the practice of devotional service.

Every morning when you wake up, remember: "This body is temporary, but I am eternal. Why should I waste my time simply maintaining this body? I must use this human form of life to revive my eternal relationship with Kṛṣṇa."

Chant the Hare Kṛṣṇa mahā-mantra regularly. This is the process given for this age to cleanse the mirror of the heart. As you chant, the covering on the soul begins to dissolve, and your real spiritual nature begins to manifest. You will gradually understand your eternal relationship with Kṛṣṇa.

Also, associate with devotees. The company you keep is very important. If you associate with materialistic persons who think "I am this body," you will also think like that. But if you associate with those who are cultivating spiritual knowledge, you will make advancement.

Avoid activities that strengthen bodily consciousness—excessive eating, sleeping, unnecessary sense gratification. Instead, engage in activities that remind you of your spiritual nature: reading Bhagavad-gītā, offering food to Kṛṣṇa, hearing about His pastimes, serving His devotees.

## Related Verses

In Bhagavad-gītā (15.7), Kṛṣṇa says:

*mamaivāṁśo jīva-loke
jīva-bhūtaḥ sanātanaḥ*

"The living entities in this conditioned world are My eternal fragmental parts."

This confirms that we are eternally connected to Kṛṣṇa. We can never be separated from Him in reality. We are His parts and parcels.

In Bhagavad-gītā (2.13), the Lord explains:

*dehino 'smin yathā dehe
kaumāraṁ yauvanaṁ jarā
tathā dehāntara-prāptir
dhīras tatra na muhyati*

"As the embodied soul continuously passes, in this body, from boyhood to youth to old age, the soul similarly passes into another body at death. A sober person is not bewildered by such a change."

This verse makes it very clear that the soul and body are different. You remember your childhood body, but that body is no longer there. The cells have all changed. Yet you, the soul, remain the same person, the same identity.

---

So my dear student, please try to understand this most important knowledge. You are not this body. You are an eternal, blissful, cognizant spirit soul, part and parcel of Kṛṣṇa. Your business is to revive your relationship with Him through devotional service. This is the perfection of human life. The body will be finished—maybe today, maybe tomorrow, maybe after one hundred years—but you will continue. Therefore, don't waste time. Take to Kṛṣṇa consciousness seriously and make your life perfect.

*Hare Kṛṣṇa!*

It takes its birth from the womb of the mother’s body, remains for some time, grows, produces some effects, gradually dwindles, and at last vanishes into oblivion. The soul, however, does not go through such changes. The soul is not born, but, because he takes on a material body, the body takes its birth. He is eternal, ever-existing and primeval – that is, there is no trace in history of his coming into being. Under the impression of the body, we seek the history of birth, etc., of the soul. The soul does not at any time become old, as the body does. The so-called old man, therefore, feels himself to be in the same spirit as in his childhood or youth. The changes of the body do not affect the soul. The soul does not deteriorate like a tree, nor anything material. The soul has no by-product either. The by-products of the body, namely children, are also different individual souls; and, owing to the body, they appear as children of a particular man. The body develops because of the soul’s presence, but the soul has neither offshoots nor change. Therefore, the soul is free from the six changes of the body.

Sometimes people are under the impression that the soul is different from the body and that when the body is finished, or one is liberated from the body, the soul remains in a void and becomes impersonal. Padma Purāṇa,
man’s sinful activities have been analyzed and are shown to be the results of sin after sin. For instance, when the seed of a particular tree is sown, the tree does not appear immediately to grow; it takes some time. It is first a small, sprouting plant, then it assumes the form of a tree, then it flowers and bears fruit, and, when it is complete, the flowers and fruits are enjoyed by persons who have sown the seed of the tree. Similarly, a man performs a sinful act, and like a seed it takes time to fructify. There are different stages.

question: How should one perform their duty?
answer_text:
My dear student, this is a most important question, for understanding how to work is the key to liberation. Let me explain this very carefully.

## Primary Teaching

In Bhagavad-gita 3.19, Lord Krishna gives the perfect instruction:

*"tasmad asaktah satatam karyam karma samacara / asakto hy acaran karma param apnoti purusah"*

**"Therefore, without being attached to the fruits of activities, one should act as a matter of duty, for by working without attachment one attains the Supreme."**

This is the secret — perform your duty without attachment to the result. You see, we are conditioned to think, "What will I get from this work?" But Krishna consciousness means we work thinking, "What does Krishna want from this work?"

In Bhagavad-gita 2.47, Krishna makes it even more clear: "You have a right to perform your prescribed duty, but you are not entitled to the fruits of action. Never consider yourself the cause of the results of your activities, and never be attached to not doing your duty."

This verse is the very foundation of karma-yoga. Your business is to work properly; the result is in Krishna's hands.

## Purport

Now, what does it mean practically to perform duty without attachment? This requires proper understanding.

First, you must know what your duty actually is. Duty means two things: your prescribed duty according to your nature and position (varnashrama-dharma), and your duty to the Supreme Lord (bhagavata-dharma). The material duty is determined by your psychophysical condition — whether you are inclined toward intellectual work, administrative work, productive work, or service work. But beyond this material duty, everyone has the spiritual duty to serve Krishna.

As stated in Bhagavad-gita 3.35: "It is far better to discharge one's prescribed duties, even though faultily, than another's duties perfectly. Destruction in the course of performing one's own duty is better than engaging in another's duties, for to follow another's path is dangerous."

You see the point? Don't imitate others. A brahmana should not try to become a warrior, and a kshatriya should not try to become a businessman. Each person has his natural propensity. When you work according to your nature but in Krishna consciousness, that is perfect.

The key is *asakti* — detachment from the result. This doesn't mean you work carelessly! No. You must work with full attention and sincerity, but you should not be anxious about success or failure. Just as a good servant performs his duty carefully but knows the result depends on the master, similarly you should work carefully but leave the result to Krishna.

In Bhagavad-gita 2.50, Krishna says: *"buddhi-yukto jahatiha ubhe sukrta-duskrte"* — "A man engaged in devotional service rids himself of both good and bad reactions even in this life." When you work for Krishna, you transcend both piety and sin. The question is not whether your work is materially successful or unsuccessful, but whether it pleases Krishna.

## Practical Application

Let me give you some practical examples from daily life.

Suppose you are a teacher. Your duty is to teach properly, preparing your lessons carefully and explaining clearly to your students. But you should not be attached to whether students appreciate you, whether you receive awards, or whether your salary increases. These are results, and they are not in your control. Your duty is simply to teach as best as you can and offer the result to Krishna.

Or suppose you are a householder with family responsibilities. Your duty is to maintain your family properly, work honestly to earn money, protect your dependents, and train your children in Krishna consciousness. But you should not think, "I am the maintainer, I am the provider." No — Krishna is the actual maintainer. You are simply His instrument. Work diligently, but understand that Krishna is ultimately responsible for the results.

Even in devotional service itself, this principle applies. You may distribute books, but you cannot control how many people will take them. You may chant on beads, but you cannot force yourself to achieve a certain ecstatic state. Your duty is to perform the service sincerely; the result is Krishna's mercy.

The secret is to remember Krishna while working. As stated in Bhagavad-gita 9.27: *"yat karosi yad asnasi yaj juhosi dadasi yat / yat tapasyasi kaunteya tat kurusva mad-arpanam"* — "Whatever you do, whatever you eat, whatever you offer or give away, and whatever austerities you perform — do that, O son of Kunti, as an offering to Me."

Make everything an offering. When you work with this consciousness — "I am doing this for Krishna" — then there is no question of attachment or anxiety. You become free even while working.

## Related Verses

Several other verses support this teaching:

**Bhagavad-gita 18.45-46** explains: "By following his qualities of work, every man can become perfect... By worship of the Lord, who is the source of all beings and who is all-pervading, a man can attain perfection through performing his own work."

**Bhagavad-gita 5.10** states: "One who performs his duty without attachment, surrendering the results unto the Supreme Lord, is unaffected by sinful action, as the lotus leaf is untouched by water."

**Srimad-Bhagavatam 3.28.2** instructs: "One should execute one's prescribed duties to the best of one's ability and avoid unauthorized duties, being satisfied with what is achieved by the grace of the Lord."

The point is consistent throughout all shastra: Do your duty sincerely, but do it for Krishna, not for your own sense gratification. This is the perfection of work.

My dear student, if you can simply understand this one principle — work without attachment to results, offering everything to Krishna — your life will be successful. You will be peaceful in both success and failure, and you will gradually advance to the platform of pure devotional service.

This is not a dry philosophy. Try it practically and you will see the result. Krishna will guide you from within, and you will feel great satisfaction even in ordinary duties.

*Hare Krishna!*

The demigods will be satisfied by proper execution of all duties, so there is no need to worship them. Let us, rather, perform our prescribed duties very nicely. Actually, one cannot be happy without executing his proper prescribed duty. One who does not, therefore, properly discharge his prescribed duties is compared to an unchaste wife. The proper prescribed duty of the
brāhmaṇas
is the study of the
Vedas;
the proper duty of the royal order, the
kṣatriyas,
is engagement in protecting the citizens; the proper duty of the
vaiśya
community is agriculture, trade and protection of the cows; and the proper duty of the
śūdras
is service to the higher classes, namely the
brāhmaṇas, kṣatriyas
and
vaiśyas.
We belong to the
vaiśya
community, and our proper duty is to farm, trade agricultural produce and protect cows or to take to banking.”

“My dear Father,” Lord Kṛṣṇa said, “there is no need to worship the demigod Indra. Everyone has to achieve the result of his own work. We can actually see that one becomes busy according to the natural tendency of his work; and according to that natural tendency, all living entities – whether human beings or demigods – achieve their respective results. All living entities achieve higher or lower bodies and create enemies, friends or neutral parties only because of their different kinds of work. One should be careful to discharge duties according to his natural instinct and not divert attention to the worship of various demigods. The demigods will be satisfied by proper execution of all duties, so there is no need to worship them. Let us, rather, perform our prescribed duties very nicely. Actually, one cannot be happy without executing his proper prescribed duty. One who does not, therefore, properly discharge his prescribed duties is compared to an unchaste wife.

Destruction in the course of performing one’s own duty is better than engaging in another’s duties, for to follow another’s path is dangerous.
Purport
One should therefore discharge his prescribed duties in full Kṛṣṇa consciousness rather than those prescribed for others. Materially, prescribed duties are duties enjoined according to one’s psychophysical condition, under the spell of the modes of material nature. Spiritual duties are as ordered by the spiritual master for the transcendental service of Kṛṣṇa. But whether material or spiritual, one should stick to his prescribed duties even up to death, rather than imitate another’s prescribed duties. Duties on the spiritual platform and duties on the material platform may be different, but the principle of following the authorized direction is always good for the performer. When one is under the spell of the modes of material nature, one should follow the prescribed rules for his particular situation and should not imitate others. For example, a
brāhmaṇa,
who is in the mode of goodness, is nonviolent, whereas a
kṣatriya,
who is in the mode of passion, is allowed to be violent. As such, for a
kṣatriya
it is better to be vanquished following the rules of violence than to imitate a
brāhmaṇa
who follows the principles of nonviolence. Everyone has to cleanse his heart by a gradual process, not abruptly. However, when one transcends the modes of material nature and is fully situated in Kṛṣṇa consciousness, he can perform anything and everything under the direction of a bona fide spiritual master. In that complete stage of Kṛṣṇa consciousness, the
kṣatriya
may act as a
brāhmaṇa,
or a
brāhmaṇa
may act as a
kṣatriya.
In the transcendental stage, the distinctions of the material world do not apply. For example, Viśvāmitra was originally a
kṣatriya,
but later on he acted as a
brāhmaṇa,
whereas Paraśurāma was a
brāhmaṇa
but later on he acted as a
kṣatriya.
Being transcendentally situated, they could do so; but as long as one is on the material platform, he must perform his duties according to the modes of material nature.

Never consider yourself the cause of the results of your activities, and never be attached to not doing your duty.

There are three considerations here: prescribed duties, capricious work, and inaction. Prescribed duties are activities enjoined in terms of one’s acquired modes of material nature. Capricious work means actions without the sanction of authority, and inaction means not performing one’s prescribed duties. The Lord advised that Arjuna not be inactive, but that he perform his prescribed duty without being attached to the result. One who is attached to the result of his work is also the cause of the action. Thus he is the enjoyer or sufferer of the result of such actions.

Such attachment never leads one to the path of salvation. Any attachment, positive or negative, is cause for bondage. Inaction is sinful. Therefore, fighting as a matter of duty was the only auspicious path of salvation for Arjuna.

ātmavic-caraṇārcanam.
Ātma-vit
means a self-realized soul or bona fide spiritual master. Unless one is self-realized and knows what his relationship with the Supersoul is, he cannot be a bona fide spiritual master. Here it is recommended that one should seek out a bona fide spiritual master and surrender unto him (
arcanam
), for by inquiring from and worshiping him one can learn spiritual activities.
The first recommendation is
sva-dharmācaraṇam.
As long as we have this material body there are various duties prescribed for us. Such duties are divided by a system of four social orders:
brāhmaṇa, kṣatriya, vaiśya
and
śūdra.
These particular duties are mentioned in the
śāstra,
and particularly in
Bhagavad-gītā.
Sva-dharmācaraṇam
means that one must discharge the prescribed duties of his particular division of society faithfully and to the best of his ability. One should not accept another’s duty. If one is born in a particular society or community, he should perform the prescribed duties for that particular division. If, however, one is fortunate enough to transcend the designation of birth in a particular society or community by being elevated to the standard of spiritual identity, then his
sva-dharma,
or duty, is solely that of serving the Supreme Personality of Godhead.

question: What is the purpose of human life?
answer_text:
My dear student, this is the most important question anyone can ask. Let me answer you very clearly and scientifically.

## Primary Teaching

The purpose of human life is to revive our eternal relationship with Krishna, the Supreme Personality of Godhead. As stated in the Srimad-Bhagavatam, "Then He created human life, which offers the conditioned soul sufficient intelligence to perceive the Absolute Truth, and became pleased." The Lord created 8,400,000 species of life, but only in human form is there sufficient intelligence to understand the Absolute Truth.

We are all eternal parts and parcels of Krishna, just as sparks are parts of fire. In Bhagavad-gita (15.7), Krishna says, *mamaivāṁśo jīva-loke jīva-bhūtaḥ sanātanaḥ* — "The living entities in this conditioned world are My eternal fragmental parts." We have forgotten this relationship from time immemorial, and due to this forgetfulness, we are transmigrating through 8,400,000 species of life — from aquatics to plants, to insects, to birds, to beasts, and finally to human form.

The human form is specifically designed by the Supreme Lord as the gateway to liberation. It is meant for understanding, "Who am I? What is my relationship with God? Why am I suffering?" Not for simply improving our eating, sleeping, mating and defending like the animals.

## Purport

You see, every species of life is engaged in four activities: eating, sleeping, defending and mating. The dog is eating, the cat is sleeping, the birds are mating, the insects are defending. These same activities are there in human life also. But if we only engage in these four principles, then what is the difference between human life and animal life? The dog also eats, but he eats from the garbage can. The human being eats on a golden plate. But the activity is the same — eating. This is not advancement.

The real purpose, as confirmed in the Vedic literature, is to control the material senses and take to Krishna consciousness. When we do this, something wonderful happens — "the Supreme Lord personally feels happiness and gradually reveals Himself to His devotee." Just imagine! The Supreme Lord, who is controlling millions of universes, becomes personally happy when even one conditioned soul tries to revive his relationship with Him.

Human life is specifically created with sufficient intelligence to perceive the Absolute Truth. The animals cannot do this. A dog cannot sit and inquire about the Absolute Truth. But you can. This intelligence is the special gift of human life, and if we don't use it, we are committing suicide — spiritual suicide.

The value of human life is therefore immense. As I have written, "a moment lost of this life means a loss of immense value." We are not getting this human form very cheaply. After evolving through 8,400,000 species, nature has awarded us this precious form. If we simply waste it in sense gratification like animals, we are committing the greatest mistake.

## Practical Application

Now, how to fulfill this purpose? Very simple process Krishna has given.

First, you must understand that you are not this body. This body is like a shirt. When the shirt is old, you change it. Similarly, the soul changes bodies. But in human life, you can understand, "I am not this body. I am eternal spirit soul, part and parcel of Krishna."

Second, engage in devotional service. Chant Hare Krishna, Hare Krishna, Krishna Krishna, Hare Hare / Hare Rama, Hare Rama, Rama Rama, Hare Hare. This is the prescribed method for this age. Simply by chanting these holy names, you revive your dormant Krishna consciousness.

Third, live according to the principles of bhakti-yoga. Follow the four regulative principles — no meat-eating, no intoxication, no illicit sex, no gambling. These restrictions are not to make life miserable; they are to free you from animal propensities.

Fourth, study Bhagavad-gita daily. Just as you take your breakfast, lunch and dinner, you must take spiritual food by reading Krishna's words. This will keep you in Krishna consciousness.

Previously in India, the intelligent class of men, the brahmanas, would go to the forest after fifty years to meditate and realize self. That was the Vedic system. After fulfilling household duties, they would voluntarily accept austerities to conquer birth and death. That is the highest occupation for man. Not to simply work hard and enjoy sense gratification.

Human life means voluntarily practicing austerities for spiritual advancement. The animals and plants are suffering due to past karma, but human beings should voluntarily accept some suffering in the form of spiritual discipline to attain eternal happiness. This is intelligence.

## Related Verses

In Bhagavad-gita (8.16), Krishna says, *ābrahma-bhuvanāl lokāḥ punar āvartino 'rjuna* — "From the highest planet in the material world down to the lowest, all are places of misery wherein repeated birth and death take place." So even if you become very wealthy, very educated, very powerful in this life, still you have to die and accept another body. This repetition must be stopped.

In Bhagavad-gita (7.19), Krishna explains, *bahūnāṁ janmanām ante jñānavān māṁ prapadyate* — "After many births and deaths, one who is actually in knowledge surrenders unto Me." So don't wait for many births. Surrender now in this life.

And in Bhagavad-gita (9.34), Krishna gives the simple formula: *man-manā bhava mad-bhakto mad-yājī māṁ namaskuru* — "Always think of Me, become My devotee, worship Me and offer your homage unto Me." This is the purpose of human life, and this is the perfection.

So my request to you is: Don't neglect this opportunity. The time allotted by the laws of nature must be utilized fully for Krishna consciousness. Otherwise, you are simply wasting this precious human form, and you will have to again enter the cycle of birth and death through 8,400,000 species.

Take to Krishna consciousness seriously. This is my humble request.

question: What should a person do when they get suicidal thoughts?
answer_text:
My dear child, this is a very serious question, and I am glad you have asked it directly. When such thoughts come to the mind, it means the person is experiencing the most intense form of material suffering. But understand this clearly: **suicide is not the solution**. It is simply jumping from one frying pan into another fire.

## Primary Teaching

In Bhagavad-gītā 2.20, Lord Kṛṣṇa explains the eternal nature of the soul:

*na jāyate mriyate vā kadācin*
*nāyaṁ bhūtvā bhavitā vā na bhūyaḥ*
*ajo nityaḥ śāśvato 'yaṁ purāṇo*
*na hanyate hanyamāne śarīre*

"For the soul there is neither birth nor death at any time. He has not come into being, does not come into being, and will not come into being. He is unborn, eternal, ever-existing and primeval. He is not slain when the body is slain."

You see, the difficulty is this: people think by destroying the body they will end their suffering. But this is ignorance. You are not the body — you are the eternal soul within the body. Just as you cannot kill the sunshine by closing your door, you cannot kill the soul by destroying the body. The soul will simply be forced to take another body, and very likely a more miserable one due to the sinful act of self-destruction.

## Purport: Understanding the Root Cause

Why do these suicidal thoughts come? Because modern materialistic civilization has no knowledge of the soul, no knowledge of God, no knowledge of life's actual purpose. People are being trained simply to gratify the senses — eat, sleep, mate, and defend. When these arrangements break down, when there is failure in relationships, economics, health, or social status, people become desperate. They think, "Life is finished." But actually, they have never even begun real life!

In the passages from our books, we see this clearly illustrated. In America, I observed how even a famous expert in social etiquette, Amy Vanderbilt, jumped from her window at age sixty-nine. In San Francisco, they had to put fences on the Golden Gate Bridge to catch people attempting suicide. In Berkeley, the university tower became known for such attempts. These are the signs of a bankrupt civilization — externally so much advancement of technology and comfort, but internally, complete poverty of spiritual understanding.

The impersonalist philosophers, when they see the misery of material existence, they think, "Let me become zero. Let me merge into the void." This is also a form of spiritual suicide. But becoming zero is neither possible nor necessary. We are eternal persons, and Kṛṣṇa is the Supreme Person. Our relationship with Him is eternal. Material life is simply a perverted reflection of that real spiritual life.

As I have explained in my books, a diseased man lying in bed, passing stool and urine, taking bitter medicine — he may think, "Let me commit suicide." But this is foolishness! The solution is not to die but to become cured. Similarly, our disease is material consciousness. The cure is Kṛṣṇa consciousness.

## Practical Application: What To Do Immediately

If you or someone you know is experiencing suicidal thoughts, here are the immediate steps:

**1. Emergency Chanting**
Begin chanting Hare Kṛṣṇa Hare Kṛṣṇa Kṛṣṇa Kṛṣṇa Hare Hare / Hare Rāma Hare Rāma Rāma Rāma Hare Hare immediately and loudly. This is not sentiment — this is practical medicine for the mind. The holy name has the power to cleanse the heart of all material contamination. Chant loudly, at least 108 times without stopping.

**2. Association of Devotees**
Immediately seek the association of Kṛṣṇa conscious persons. Do not remain alone. Come to the temple, call devotees, speak with spiritual guides. In Bhagavad-gītā 9.30, Kṛṣṇa says even if one is the most sinful, if he properly worships Him, he should be considered saintly. There is no condition so fallen that Kṛṣṇa's mercy cannot reach it.

**3. Read Bhagavad-gītā**
Particularly read Chapter Two, which explains the eternality of the soul. Understanding "I am not this body, I am spirit soul" is the beginning of all relief. The body may be in difficulty, but you — the real you — are eternal, blissful, and full of knowledge by nature.

**4. Remember: This Too Shall Pass**
All material situations are temporary. In Bhagavad-gītā 2.14, Kṛṣṇa instructs: *mātrā-sparśās tu kaunteya śītoṣṇa-sukha-duḥkha-dāḥ*. Material happiness and distress arise from sense perception and are temporary. They come and go like winter and summer seasons. One must learn to tolerate them without being disturbed.

**5. See the Opportunity**
Every danger, every difficulty can become an impetus to remember Kṛṣṇa. As stated in our literature, if danger gives us the opportunity to remember Kṛṣṇa, that is actually welcome! Why? Because remembering Kṛṣṇa means advancing spiritually, and when we advance spiritually, we eventually transcend all suffering permanently. *Tyaktvā dehaṁ punar janma naiti mām eti so 'rjuna* (Bhagavad-gītā 4.9) — after leaving this body, one who knows Kṛṣṇa does not take birth again but comes to Him.

**6. Understand The Illusion**
We are attached even to miserable bodies. A hog eating stool thinks he is happy. When Lord Brahmā came to rescue Lord Indra from his cursed life as a hog, Indra did not want to leave! He said, "Why should I go? I have my hog wife, my hog children, I am very happy here!" This is the power of illusion. Even in the most degraded condition, we think we are happy. So if this illusion can make a hog happy, imagine how happy you can become by cultivating Kṛṣṇa consciousness in a human body!

## Related Verses and Deeper Understanding

**Bhagavad-gītā 18.66** provides the ultimate solution:
*sarva-dharmān parityajya mām ekaṁ śaraṇaṁ vraja*
*ahaṁ tvāṁ sarva-pāpebhyo mokṣayiṣyāmi mā śucaḥ*

"Abandon all varieties of religion and just surrender unto Me. I shall deliver you from all sinful reactions. Do not fear."

Whatever has brought you to this point — whatever mistakes, whatever failures, whatever sins — Kṛṣṇa promises to deliver you if you simply surrender to Him. "Do not fear," He says. This is the most important instruction.

**Bhagavad-gītā 6.22** describes the goal:
*yaṁ labdhvā cāparaṁ lābhaṁ manyate nādhikaṁ tataḥ*

"Upon gaining this, one thinks there is no greater gain." When you taste real spiritual happiness, material misery becomes insignificant. The problem is not your external situation — the problem is you have not yet tasted real happiness. You are trying to be happy by adjusting matter, but happiness comes from spirit.

## Final Words

My dear child, please understand: your life is valuable. You are an eternal servant of Kṛṣṇa, and He has a plan for you. This human form of life is very rare — out of 8,400,000 species, only in human life can we understand God and end the cycle of birth and death. To destroy this opportunity is the greatest foolishness.

I have seen in my travels throughout the world that wherever there is godlessness, there is suicide, madness, and hopelessness. But wherever people take to Kṛṣṇa consciousness, there is happiness, purpose, and hope. This is not theory — this is practical fact.

So begin immediately: chant Hare Kṛṣṇa, read Bhagavad-g

Suppose I am very comfortable at the present moment. My body may be comfortable, but there will be death, and then another birth. After giving up my present body, if I get the body of a cat or a dog, what is the meaning of my comfortable position? Death is sure, and after death one must surely accept another body. We may not know what kind of body we shall get, but we can know from the
śāstra,
the Vedic literature. The
śāstra
says that according to our particular mentality, we will get a particular kind of body. Although I may be in a comfortable position, if I keep myself in the mentality of a dog, I shall get my next life as a dog. Therefore, what is the value of this comfortable position? I may be in a comfortable position for twenty years, thirty years, fifty years, or at the utmost one hundred years. Yet if, when I give up this body, my mentality causes me to become a cat, a dog, or a mouse, what is the benefit of this comfortable position? But people do not consider this. They think, especially in the present age, “I am now in a comfortable position. I have enough money and a good estate. I have ample comforts and enough food. When this body is finished, I am not going to take birth again, so as long as I am living, let me enjoy life.” This is the modern philosophy of hedonism, but it does not correspond to the facts.

Suppose I am very comfortable at the present moment. My body may be comfortable, but there will be death, and then another birth.

No one thinks that he is going to die, although everyone else is dying.
The living entity thinks that he is very happy in whatever body he gets. He may get the body of a dog or the body of a cat, but he is happy in his body, and he does not want to lose it. This is called illusion. When an ant sees danger, he will run like anything. This is because he values his body and does not want to lose it. Once Lord Indra, the king of the heavenly planets, was cursed by Bṛhaspati to become a hog. He was going about on earth enjoying his hog body when finally Lord Brahmā came and told him, “My dear Indra, you have suffered enough. Now come with me and return to your heavenly kingdom.” Indra in the form of a hog said, “Where shall I go?” “To the heavens,” Lord Brahmā replied. It was then that Indra said, “No, I have my family and children. How can I go? I am very happy. Let me remain here.” In this way, everyone is thinking that he is very happy, although he is in a horrible and fearsome condition. However, one who is actually intelligent can understand that he is not actually happy, that he is suffering. It is said that ignorance is bliss, but this is the bliss of a hog. When one actually comes to the understanding that he is not actually happy in the material world, he can begin to relieve his suffering.
There is no possibility of being liberated from suffering without taking shelter at Kṛṣṇa’s lotus feet. People are thinking that they can relieve suffering through abortion, by killing the child within the womb. In this way they are committing one sin after another and becoming more and more entangled. Consequently the aborted living entity will have to enter into the womb of another mother in order to undergo the birth that he is destined to take. Then, when he enters the womb of another mother, he may again be killed, and for many years he may not be allowed to see the light of the sun. In this Kali-yuga, people are becoming so sinful that there is no possibility of rescue unless one takes to Kṛṣṇa consciousness.

question: How can someone be saved from addictions?
answer_text:
My dear student, this is a very important question for modern society. The problem of addiction — whether to drugs, intoxication, illicit sex, or any other material habit — is actually a spiritual disease requiring a spiritual cure. Let me explain this very clearly.

## Primary Teaching

In Bhagavad-gita 7.14, Lord Krishna explains the root cause and the only solution:

*"This divine energy of Mine, consisting of the three modes of material nature, is difficult to overcome. But those who have surrendered unto Me can easily cross beyond it."*

You see, addiction is not simply a physical or psychological problem. It is the symptom of being trapped in maya, the illusory energy of Krishna. The material energy binds us through the three modes — goodness, passion, and ignorance — and in this age of Kali, the mode of ignorance is very strong. This is why people are becoming addicted to so many sinful activities.

The passages you have received mention four main addictions that plague conditioned souls:
1. Illegitimate connection with women (or men)
2. Animal killing and meat-eating
3. Intoxication habits
4. Gambling

These are symptoms of spiritual disease. And what is the cure? Krishna consciousness — specifically the chanting of the Hare Krishna maha-mantra. This is not my invention. This is the prescription given by Sri Chaitanya Mahaprabhu for this age.

## Purport

Now, you may ask, "Prabhupada, why does chanting work when other methods fail?" This is a very good question. Let me explain.

When we perform ordinary pious activities — giving charity, performing rituals, even undergoing austerity — we can temporarily counteract sinful reactions. Just like if you have some disease, you take medicine and the symptoms go away for some time. But the disease-seed remains in the heart. As soon as the opportunity comes, the addiction returns, perhaps even stronger than before.

I have seen this personally in the West. Young people try to give up drugs through willpower or rehabilitation programs. For some time they may be successful, but unless the **desire-seed** is removed from the heart, they fall down again. Why? Because the heart is still dirty. The material desires are still there, waiting.

But Krishna consciousness works differently. When you chant Hare Krishna Hare Krishna Krishna Krishna Hare Hare, Hare Rama Hare Rama Rama Rama Hare Hare, you are calling out to the Supreme Personality of Godhead. This transcendental sound vibration has the power to cleanse the mirror of the heart completely. Not just temporarily — permanently.

In Srimad-Bhagavatam, the great devotee Sukadeva Gosvami explains that unless one adopts the path of devotional service, one cannot be one-hundred-percent clean from all reactions of sinful activities. This is confirmed. Other processes give partial relief, but only bhakti-yoga, devotional service, removes the root cause.

I have told this to many young people in America. I said, "America is finished with this drug habit. Your so-called independence has spoiled everything. But there is one shelter — Krishna consciousness." And I have seen the proof! Boys and girls who were completely addicted to heroin, LSD, and other terrible drugs — they came to our temple, began chanting Hare Krishna, taking Krishna prasadam, and within a short time, all their addiction disappeared. Not by force, not by struggle, but naturally, because they found something higher.

This is the secret: you must replace the lower taste with a higher taste. In Bhagavad-gita 2.59, Krishna explains: *"The embodied soul may be restricted from sense enjoyment, though the taste for sense objects remains. But, ceasing such engagements by experiencing a higher taste, he is fixed in consciousness."*

A child is sucking on some broken glass — dangerous! The mother cannot simply pull it away; the child will cry and become angry. But if she offers something better — some sweet candy — the child naturally gives up the glass. Similarly, material enjoyment is like broken glass. It appears pleasing but cuts us deeply. Krishna consciousness is the higher taste that makes all material addiction insignificant.

## Practical Application

Now, how do you apply this teaching? Here are the practical steps:

**First**, you must take shelter of the holy name. Chant Hare Krishna daily, at least 16 rounds on beads if you are serious. This is not difficult. If you can smoke cigarettes or marijuana for hours, you can certainly chant for one or two hours daily.

**Second**, take Krishna prasadam regularly. Food offered to Krishna becomes spiritualized. When you eat such food, your consciousness gradually changes. The body and mind become purified. I have seen this works wonderfully. In our temples, we always distribute prasadam freely. This is transcendental treatment.

**Third**, associate with devotees. This is very important. If you keep association with drug addicts, drunkards, or those engaged in illicit activities, you will be pulled down. But if you associate with those who are chanting, dancing, and serving Krishna, you will be lifted up. It is said, *"A man is known by his company."* Change your company, change your life.

**Fourth**, engage in devotional service. An idle mind is the devil's workshop. When you are engaged in cooking for Krishna, cleaning the temple, distributing books, studying scripture — where is time for addiction? The mind becomes absorbed in transcendental activities and forgets material cravings.

**Fifth**, follow the regulative principles: no meat-eating, no intoxication, no illicit sex, no gambling. These four regulations are like four pillars supporting your spiritual life. They protect you from falling back into maya's grip.

You must understand, this is a complete lifestyle change, not just a part-time hobby. Just like if you are drowning in the ocean, you don't hold the lifeguard's hand casually. You grab on for dear life! Similarly, this material world is an ocean of birth and death. Krishna consciousness is the lifeguard. Hold on tightly!

## Related Verses

Several other verses support this teaching:

**Bhagavad-gita 9.30-31**: "Even if one commits the most abominable action, if he is engaged in devotional service he is to be considered saintly because he is properly situated in his determination. He quickly becomes righteous and attains lasting peace."

This is very encouraging! Even if you have been addicted to the worst habits, if you sincerely take to Krishna consciousness, you will be purified. Krishna Himself guarantees this.

**Bhagavad-gita 18.66**: "Abandon all varieties of religion and just surrender unto Me. I shall deliver you from all sinful reactions. Do not fear."

Complete surrender is the ultimate solution. When you surrender to Krishna, He personally takes charge of your purification. This is not sentiment — this is fact.

**Srimad-Bhagavatam 6.2.7**: The story of Ajamila demonstrates that even at the moment of death, by calling out the name of Narayana (Krishna), one can be saved from the most sinful life. If this is possible at death, how much more powerful is the holy name when chanted regularly throughout life!

My final advice: Don't delay. Take up this Krishna consciousness movement immediately. The hippies are searching for peace in drugs and free living, but they are searching in the wrong place. The peace they want is available in New Vrindaban, in our temples, in Krishna consciousness. Come and test this process yourself. Chant Hare Krishna and see the result. Your life will change completely.

Thank you very much. Hare Krishna!

All Americans, the so-called puffed up materialist. This is a fact. So if you want to do service to your country, introduce this Kṛṣṇa consciousness to the younger generation. They will be saved. Prabhupāda: Just like in hog civilization. That's all. They are thinking that "We are very happy eating stool." But others know that what kind of happiness he is enjoying. Similarly these rascal karmis, materialists, they are thinking by... What is called? Kini...What is called? Naked skirt? Devotees: Miniskirt? Prabhupāda: Miniskirt, trying to show the private part and people will be attracted and she will be happy. This is regular prostitution. Regular prostitution. Still they are not getting husband. Even they walk naked... That will come. Say after fifty years it will come like that.

Pradyumna and Vamanadeva are very good boys; keep them very nicely, and they will be very much helpful. I have come here to Seattle, last evening, and things are hopeful here also. Yesterday, before coming here I was talking with one hippy boy. He came to see me in my apartment in San Francisco, and was talking that hippies are in search after such peaceful place, like you have got in New Vrindaban. And they do not like to live in the cities anymore. So if some of us go and make some canvassing work among the hippies, that the thing which they are searching after is here in Krishna Consciousness, and as soon as they come they will understand, so somebody may be engaged in such propaganda work. Regarding Randy, I understand that he is addicted to heroin intoxication, and he is taking some medicine to give it up, but I am sure that the best medicine is chanting the HARE KRISHNA mantra. For the time being, let him take the substitute medicine as he is taking, and at the same time let him seriously chant HARE KRISHNA. And I hope he will be able to give up that substitute medicine also. I have got experience—one of my God-brothers, he related about his past life, that he was addicted to all kinds of intoxication, and he was distilling wine in his village without any license—he was such a great intoxicant. But later on, we found him completely free from all intoxication—even he was not even smoking and was quite fit to work for Krishna Consciousness. So everything is possible provided one is serious. In the Srimad-Bhagavatam it is stated that if somebody is not too much addicted, neither he is completely powerful to give up some bad habits, this via media stage is favorable. Immediately if he cannot give up the medicine as he is taking, that doesn't matter. Let him chant HARE KRISHNA very seriously and he will gradually be able to give up everything.
I am very glad to learn that Harivilasa has given you a Pair of Radha Krishna Murtis, so it appears that Radha Krishna is very kind on you, because you went to Vrindaban, to live there but circumstantially, you could not live there, and you left.

Instead of spending money to advance the cause of Krishna consciousness, one spends his hard earned money on clubs, brothels, liquor, drugs cigarettes, slaughterhouses and so forth. All these activities are sinful, and because we are engaged in such sinful activities constantly we are constantly reaping the result of our sinful work, which is suffering in one way or another.
But even if one is pious he cannot escape from the sufferings of material existence in the shape of Birth, death, old age and disease. Therefore the only solution to all problems of life social economic, or political is to surrender to Krishna. Krishna says "the material nature is very difficult to overcome, but one who has surrendered unto me can very easily cross beyond it." So to surrender to Krishna is not difficult all we have to do is follow the instructions given by Krishna in Bhagavad-gita under the guidance of his representative, the spiritual master.
So you are already chanting Hare Krishna, now simply try to give up these four habits of sinful life, and the best way to be successful in this attempt is to associate with the devotees of the Lord. Therefore we have established so many temples of Krishna. You simply go there, the devotees are very kind, you will be welcome and by their association automatically you will make advancement and purify your life. We have got a temple in London, 7 Bury Place WC I and one in Herts. Given to us by George Harrison. So you go there and everything else will come.
I hope this letter finds you well.
Your ever well wisher,
A.C. Bhaktivedanta Swami

Śukadeva points out that austerity, charity and the performance of ritualistic ceremonies for counteracting sinful activities are recommended processes, but that by performing them one cannot remove the sinful desire-seed from the heart, as was the case with Ajāmila in his youth. This sinful desire-seed can be removed only by achieving Kṛṣṇa consciousness. And this can be accomplished very easily by chanting the
mahā-mantra,
or Hare Kṛṣṇa
mantra,
as recommended by Śrī Caitanya Mahāprabhu. In other words, unless one adopts the path of devotional service, he cannot be one-hundred-percent clean from all the reactions of sinful activities.

By performing Vedic ritualistic activities, by giving money in charity and by undergoing austerity, one can temporarily become free from the reactions of sinful activities, but at the next moment he must again become engaged in sinful activities. For example, a person suffering from venereal disease on account of excessive indulgence in sex life has to undergo some severe pain in medical treatment, and he is then cured for the time being. But because he has not been able to remove the sex desire from his heart, he must again indulge in the same thing and become a victim of the same disease. So medical treatment may give temporary relief from the distress of such venereal disease, but unless one is trained to understand that sex life is abominable, it is impossible to be saved from such repeated distress. Similarly, the ritualistic performances, charity and austerity that are recommended in the
Vedas
may temporarily stop one from acting in sinful ways, but as long as the heart is not clear, one will have to repeat sinful activities again and again.

the mirror of the heart of human being becomes cleansed of all the above mentioned dusts. As soon as the dusts are removed the human being is able to visualize a clear conception of his real self and thereby he becomes free from the miseries pertaining to the body & mind, pertaining to the other living being and pertaining to the onslaughts of nature.
As followers of Bhagavad-gita we are firmly convinced that the onslaught of nature which is constantly being inflicted upon human society is a sort of police action by the laws of the Almighty and as soon as we surrender unto the Divine Will of the Almighty we not only become Mahatmas & saints but also the attack of unkind Nature is vanquished at once. That is the teaching of Bhagavad-gita and that is the demonstrative preaching of Lord Caitanya Mahaprabhu. The real life begins when we are freed from the clutches of Maya which is nothing but a bewildered conception of materialistic life. Sri Caitanya Mahaprabhu changed the face of it spiritually.
In the Bhagavad-gita it is said that the common folk follows the leading men of society. At the present moment of democratic age the Government of the State is conducted by the leading men of society. As such it is a good sign that the Government has taken up this work of social upliftment as a right measure at a right time. And in order to give the scheme a right direction the government may take authoritative hints from the scriptures like Bhagavad-gita.
